ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Turkish butter rice (tereyağlı pilav). Mapped to the broader 'rice dish' class (FOODON_00002694) as the closest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code for tereyağlı pilav. A000E (composite rice dish) used as the closest defensible broader category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Turkish butter rice or pilaf as a specific food entity.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving of Turkish Butter Rice is approximately 180g (about one medium bowl), providing roughly 295 kcal.

nutrient_source.title: Turkish Butter Rice (Tereyağlı Pilav) — HPB Singapore Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Turkish Butter Rice (cr_id G00010163), which records this dish as rice cooked with butter and barley pasta (arpa şehriye). All per-100g values are taken from this canonical HPB record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — white rice (boiled, general)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Turkish Butter Rice (tereyağlı pilav). GI estimated at 69 based on the closest analogous food: boiled white rice (GI ~69–72, medium category) from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care). The addition of butter slightly lowers the effective GI by slowing gastric emptying, but the dominant carbohydrate source remains white rice and orzo (both moderate-to-high GI starchy foods). GI 69 is used as a conservative medium-range estimate. Glycemic load per 180g serving = (69 × 25.19 × 1.8) / 100 = 31.3, rounded to 31.2.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 231 mg/100g is in the borderline range (200–400 mg); however, potassium at 327 mg/100g exceeds sodium and dietary fibre at 3.7 g/100g meets the ≥3 g offset threshold, partially mitigating the sodium concern under DASH principles.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 150 g and use unsalted low-sodium stock in preparation to keep total sodium intake below 200 mg per serving; the favourable potassium-to-sodium ratio provides partial cardiovascular offset.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate at 25.2 g/100g with an estimated GI of 69 (medium) yields a high glycemic load per standard serving (~31 GL units); white rice and orzo are rapidly digestible starches that elevate postprandial blood glucose.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should restrict portions to 100–120 g cooked, consider substituting white rice with parboiled or basmati rice to lower GI, and pair the dish with non-starchy vegetables and a protein source to blunt the postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Butter contributes approximately 2.5 g saturated fat per 100g, which is below the 5 g threshold but still relevant for cardiovascular risk when consumed in regular portions; cholesterol is reported at 0 mg/100g by the HPB source.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit serving size to 150 g, substitute butter with extra-virgin olive oil to reduce saturated fat and introduce cardioprotective monounsaturated fatty acids, and monitor overall daily saturated fat intake from all sources.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Butter is the primary fat source and provides no meaningful anti-inflammatory factors (no omega-3, polyphenols, or qualifying whole-food fibre); the dish lacks any positive anti-inflammatory nutrient profile required for RA suitability.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should substitute butter with extra-virgin olive oil and add anti-inflammatory herbs such as turmeric or garlic to introduce polyphenols and oleocanthal that may help modulate inflammatory pathways.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Turkish Butter Rice, known in Turkish as Tereyağlı Pilav or Şehriyeli Pilav, is a classic Turkish rice dish prepared by toasting arpa şehriye (orzo or barley-shaped pasta) in butter until golden, then adding white rice and cooking together in salted water or stock until fully absorbed. The result is a fluffy, lightly nutty pilaf with a rich buttery aroma and a tender texture from the combination of soft rice grains and slightly firmer orzo pieces. It is a staple side dish in Turkish cuisine, commonly served alongside grilled meats, stews, and kebabs, and is widely available in Turkish restaurants in Singapore and across the Middle Eastern diaspora. Nutritionally, per 100g it provides 164 kcal, 6.5 g protein, 4.21 g fat (approximately 2.5 g saturated from butter), 25.2 g carbohydrate, 3.7 g dietary fibre, and 231 mg sodium. The moderate-to-high glycaemic load makes portion control important for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. The borderline sodium level is partially offset by a favourable potassium-to-sodium ratio (327 mg vs 231 mg). The dish is vegetarian and halal but contains gluten (from orzo) and dairy (from butter), making it unsuitable for those with coeliac disease or lactose intolerance. Substituting butter with olive oil would improve the anti-inflammatory and cardiovascular profile of this dish.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010163) with a recorded confidence of 0.95. The HPB is an approved primary regional source for Singapore food records. The dish identity, description, and ingredient composition are consistent with the canonical Turkish preparation of tereyağlı pilav.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient vitamins, phosphorus, zinc, selenium, and moisture data for this specific Turkish butter rice preparation are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry (G00010163) as of 2026-04-16. saturated_fat_g estimated at 2.5 g/100g based on typical butter contribution (~14g fat per 100g butter, ~63% saturated) proportioned to the 4.21g total fat reported; this is an approximation as HPB does not report saturated fat separately for this entry. cholesterol_mg is reported as 0.0 by the HPB source; this is confirmed zero per the HPB record despite butter being present, likely reflecting the small quantity of butter per 100g of the cooked dish. GI of 69 is an analogous estimate based on boiled white rice from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care); no direct GI measurement for tereyağlı pilav was found in approved sources. This is a Turkish dish served in Singapore; multilingual naming rules for Singaporean dishes (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) do not apply as the dish is of Turkish origin and not a Singaporean-origin dish. Romanized Turkish name (Tereyağlı Pilav) is provided as the primary local-language form.
